elements.sent-2411-001: Deep learning has achieved remarkable success in image classification and segmentation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, fairness concerns persist, as models often exhibit biases that disproportionately affect demographic groups defined by sensitive attributes such as race, gender, or age.
elements.sent-2411-003: Existing bias-mitigation techniques, including Subgroup Re-balancing, Adversarial Training, and Domain Generalization, aim to balance accuracy across demographic groups, but often fail to simultaneously improve overall accuracy, group-specific accuracy, and fairness due to conflicts among these interdependent objectives.
elements.sent-2411-004: We propose the Fair Distillation (FairDi) method, a novel fairness approach that decomposes these objectives by leveraging biased ``teacher'' models, each optimized for a specific demographic group.
elements.sent-2411-005: These teacher models then guide the training of a unified ``student'' model, which distills their knowledge to maximize overall and group-specific accuracies, while minimizing inter-group disparities.
elements.sent-2411-006: Experiments on medical imaging datasets show that FairDi achieves significant gains in both overall and group-specific accuracy, along with improved fairness, compared to existing methods.
elements.sent-2411-007: FairDi is adaptable to various medical tasks, such as classification and segmentation, and provides an effective solution for equitable model performance.
elements.sent-2411-008: In recent years, deep learning models have made remarkable progress, offering automated solutions for tasks such as classification and segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-009: However, as these models transition into practice, concerns regarding fairness have emerged~\cite{obermeyer2019dissecting, mehrabi2021survey, kadambi2021achieving, chen2021ethical, chen2023algorithmic .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Machine learning models, particularly those used in healthcare, have been shown to exhibit biases toward demographic groups defined by sensitive attributes such as race, gender, and age~\cite{zong2022medfair, dutt2023fairtune, han2024ffb .}
elements.sent-2411-011: These biases can lead to unfair treatment of specific groups, exacerbating existing disparities.
elements.sent-2411-012: Addressing these biases is not only a technical challenge but also a moral and ethical imperative to ensure equitable healthcare for all individuals.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figures/Motivation.png} \caption{Performance comparison of models (ERM~\cite{vapnik1999overview}, GroupDRO~\cite{sagawa2019distributionally}, SWAD~\cite{cha2021swad}, FIS~\cite{luo2024fairvisionequitabledeeplearning}, and our FairDi) on the HAM10000 dataset~\cite{tschandl2018ham10000} for benign/malignant classification by gender. The left panel shows group-specific AUCs (Male and Female), and the right panel plots fairness (AUC Gap) vs. Overall AUC. Each model's Pareto front includes two points: one maximizing worst-group AUC, the other maximizing overall AUC. Our FairDi achieves the best balance with high overall AUC, low AUC Gap, and robust group-specific AUCs.} \label{fig:motivation}
elements.sent-2411-013: Current bias mitigation approaches~\cite{nitesh2002smote, idrissi2022simple,madras2018learning, zhao2019conditional,tartaglione2021end,sarhan2020fairness,cha2021swad, sagawa2019distributionally,foret2020sharpness aim to reduce performance disparities across sensitive groups by modifying the training process or adjusting data distributions.}
elements.sent-2411-014: These methods typically seek to maximize empirical accuracy for each sensitive group, while also improving overall accuracy and minimizing accuracy gaps between groups.
elements.sent-2411-015: Consequently, they can be formulated as multi-objective optimization problems~\cite{martinez2020minimax , with solutions designed to achieve optimal trade-offs among conflicting objectives.}
elements.sent-2411-016: These trade-offs form a set of non-dominated solutions, which are superior to all other solutions in the search space but cannot be further improved in one objective without compromising another.
elements.sent-2411-017: In this context, each fairness method generates a unique Pareto front, representing its best possible balance among objectives such as overall accuracy and inter-group fairness~\cite{zong2022medfair, MasColell1995microeconomic, martinez2020minimax .}
elements.sent-2411-018: The left panel illustrates group-specific AUCs (Male and Female), while the right panel plots AUC Gap versus Overall AUC.
elements.sent-2411-019: Each model’s Pareto front includes points for maximizing either worst-group or overall AUC, demonstrating trade-offs between fairness and accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-020: Unlike prior methods, which typically sacrifice overall or group-specific AUC for fairness, FairDi applies a novel decomposed optimization of the Pareto front, allowing it to achieve a superior balance, with high overall AUC, low AUC Gap, and robust group-specific AUCs, outperforming other methods on these metrics.
elements.sent-2411-021: In this paper, we introduce the first fairness method, titled Fair Distillation (FairDi), which is specifically designed to decompose the objectives of maximizing accuracy for each sensitive group from maximizing overall accuracy and minimizing inter-group accuracy gaps.
elements.sent-2411-022: This objective decomposition is achieved in two stages: first, we train highly biased models, each optimized to maximize accuracy within a specific sensitive group.
elements.sent-2411-023: These models then serve as ``teachers'' for a unified ``student'' model, which distills knowledge from the biased ``teachers'', while simultaneously training to maximize overall accuracy and reduce accuracy gaps across groups, effectively balancing fairness and performance.
elements.sent-2411-024: The main contributions of this paper are:.
elements.sent-2411-025: Introduction of the first fairness method, called FairDi, that decomposes accuracy maximization for each sensitive group from overall accuracy maximization and inter-group accuracy gap minimization, with a teacher-student model, where biased teachers are trained for specific groups, while a single student distills their knowledge, maximizing overall accuracy and reducing accuracy gaps across groups;.
elements.sent-2411-026: Proposal of a new teacher-student training algorithm that is highly adaptable to various datasets and both classification and segmentation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-027: Comprehensive evaluations across diverse medical imaging datasets show that FairDi significantly outperforms state-of-the-art fairness methods, achieving higher overall and group-specific accuracies while minimizing inter-group disparities.
elements.sent-2411-028: Its adaptability to various tasks, including classification and segmentation, underscores its potential to advance equitable AI in critical applications.
elements.sent-2411-029: Fairness in Artificial Intelligence (AI), particularly in healthcare, has gained increasing attention as machine learning models have been shown to exhibit biases related to sensitive attributes such as race, gender, and age~\cite{quadrianto2019discovering,ramaswamy2021fair,zhang2020towards,park2022fair,roh2020fr,sarhan2020fairness,zafar2017fairness,zhang2018mitigating,wang2022fairness,kim2019multiaccuracy,lohia2019bias,tian2024fairdomain,tian2024fairseg,luo2024fairclip,luo2023harvard,luo2023glau_fair,luo2023eye,zong2022medfair,shi2023equitable,shi2024equitable .}
elements.sent-2411-030: Studies by \citet{obermeyer2019dissecting and \citet{larrazabal2020gender highlight how biases in healthcare models can lead to disparities in outcomes, particularly in underrepresented populations, making fairness an essential concern in the development of AI systems for healthcare.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Bias mitigation techniques to address fairness concerns can be categorized into pre-processing, in-processing, and post-processing strategies.
elements.sent-2411-032: Pre-processing methods, like SMOTE~\cite{nitesh2002smote , aim to re-balance data before training, while post-processing approaches~\cite{pleiss2017fairness adjust model predictions based on sensitive attributes after training.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Focusing on in-processing methods, one prominent method is Adversarial Training~\cite{zhang2018mitigating, kim2019learning , which minimizes bias by optimizing the model to perform well on the classification task while reducing its ability to predict sensitive attributes.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Despite their success, these methods often encounter a trade-off between fairness and model performance~\cite{madras2018learning, zhao2019conditional .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Another notable in-processing method is Group Distributionally Robust Optimization (GroupDRO)~\cite{sagawa2019distributionally that focuses on minimizing worst-case performance across demographic groups.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Although GroupDRO ensures fairness across underperforming groups, it may reduce overall model performance~\cite{zong2022medfair .}
elements.sent-2411-037: Domain generalization techniques, e.g., Stochastic Weight Averaging Densely (SWAD)~\cite{cha2021swad , represent in-processing methods that have shown promising results, with a training process that finds flat minima in the loss landscape.}
elements.sent-2411-038: However, despite its generally robust performance, SWAD may inadvertently widen disparities between subgroups, causing it to not significantly outperform the baseline Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) method, raising questions about its suitability as a standalone solution for fairness~\cite{zong2022medfair .}
elements.sent-2411-039: Another relevant in-processing method is based on representation disentanglement approaches that aim to separate sensitive attributes from task-relevant information in the learned representations.
elements.sent-2411-040: However, while disentanglement can be effective in isolating individual sources of bias, it often underperforms in complex tasks where multiple biases coexist.
elements.sent-2411-041: This narrow focus limits generalization and efficacy, where confounding factors are deeply intertwined and challenging to separate.
elements.sent-2411-042: Recent fairness methods introduce bias mitigation approaches to address group fairness in conjunction with overall accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-043: For instance, FairSeg~\cite{tian2024fairseg proposes Fair Error-Bound Scaling, which adjusts loss functions to focus on the hardest cases within each demographic group, ensuring equitable performance across different sensitive attributes.}
elements.sent-2411-044: FairCLIP~\cite{luo2024fairclip reduces bias in vision-language models using an optimal transport-based method to align demographic distributions, while FairVision~\cite{luo2024fairvisionequitabledeeplearning introduces Fair Identity Scaling (FIS) to improve fairness in both 2D and 3D medical imaging.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: Importantly, all these methods utilize a single model, which often suffers from the conflicting objectives of group fairness and overall performance.
elements.sent-2411-046: In contrast, our approach employs cohort-specific teacher models that optimize performance independently for each cohort before distilling their knowledge into a unified student model, thus addressing fairness across groups without compromising overall performance.
elements.sent-2411-047: Knowledge distillation has become a powerful technique in machine learning, with significant applications across various domains~\cite{gou2021knowledge .}
elements.sent-2411-048: Firstly introduced by~\citet{hinton2015distilling,buciluǎ2006model , knowledge distillation is widely used for model compression~\cite{sanh2019distilbert , semi-supervised learning~\cite{tarvainen2017mean , and multi-modal learning~\cite{lu2019vilbert .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: We propose an extension of knowledge distillation to the realm of fairness in AI.
elements.sent-2411-050: By leveraging knowledge distillation to transfer cohort-specific knowledge from multiple teacher models to a unified student model, we present the first approach to utilize knowledge distillation explicitly for achieving fairness across demographic groups and optimizing model overall performance.
elements.sent-2411-051: Our proposed method addresses fairness in medical image analysis by implementing a student-teacher knowledge-distillation framework designed to balance cohort-specific accuracy with overall accuracy and fairness among demographic groups, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:algorithm .}
elements.sent-2411-052: The approach begins by pre-training a backbone model on the full dataset using a loss function~\cite{luo2024fairvisionequitabledeeplearning that jointly optimizes the conflicting objectives of overall accuracy and fairness (Step 0).}
elements.sent-2411-053: While this initial pre-training struggles to achieve an ideal balance between these objectives, it yields a robust backbone model that serves as a strong foundation for the subsequent cohort-specific teacher models and the unified student model.
elements.sent-2411-054: We then create one teacher model per demographic cohort (e.g., male or female) using the pre-trained backbone, which is greedily fine-tuned to optimize cohort-specific accuracy (Step 1).
elements.sent-2411-055: In the final stage (Step 2), our framework distills knowledge from the biased teacher models into a single student model, aiming to jointly optimize group-specific and overall accuracies together with fairness.
elements.sent-2411-056: This approach seeks to achieve the highest possible cohort-specific accuracies while also maximizing overall accuracy and fairness for the resulting model.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figures/FairDi.png} \caption{Diagram of the FairDi training process. After pre-training (step 0), it optimizes the teachers' performances for their respective sensitive groups (step 1), followed by a knowledge distillation process that trains a single student to optimize overall and group-specific accuracies, while minimizing performance gap between groups (step 2). The \textcolor{red}{flame} symbol indicates model components to be trained during each corresponding step, while the \textcolor{blue}{snowflake} symbol represents components that remain frozen.} \label{fig:algorithm}

elements.sent-2411-058: Since we use stochastic gradient descent (or a variant) for optimization, $\mathcal{D}$ in the formulations below denotes a mini-batch rather than the entire training set.
elements.sent-2411-059: The backbone model transforms a sample from the image space to the feature space $\mathcal{F}$ with $f_{\theta :\mathcal{X \to \mathcal{F}$ parameterized by $\theta \in \Theta$, followed by a temperature-scaled prediction layer, defined by $h_{\phi,\tau :\mathcal{F \to [0,1]^{Y}$ for classification and $h_{\phi,\tau :\mathcal{F \to [0,1]^{W \times H \times Y}$ for segmentation, and parameterized by weights $\phi \in \Phi$ and temperature $\tau \in \mathcal{T \subset \mathbb{R}$.}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-061: The backbone model and classification layer are trained using the Fair Identity Scaling (FIS) loss function~\cite{luo2024fairvisionequitabledeeplearning , which is designed to balance individual and group-level fairness, with \begin{equation}0.9{$ \theta^*,\phi^* = \arg\min\limits_{\theta,\phi} \frac{1}{N} \sum\limits_{ (x,y,a) \in \mathcal{D}} \mathsf{w}(x,y,a,c) \times \ell \left (h_{\phi} \left ( f_{\theta}(x)\right) ,y \right ), $} \label{eq:fis_optimization}\end{equation} where \( \ell(h_{\phi \left ( f_{\theta (x)\right), y) \) is the classification loss and $\mathsf{w (x,y,a,c) = \left ( (1-c) \times \mathsf{s ^{I (x,y) + c \times \mathsf{s ^{G (a) ) \right )$, with $\mathsf{s ^{I (x,y) = \frac{\exp(\ell(h_{\phi \left ( f_{\theta (x)\right), y)) {\sum\limits_{ (\tilde{x ,\tilde{y ,\tilde{a ) \in \mathcal{D \exp(\ell(h_{\phi \left ( f_{\theta (\tilde{x )\right), \tilde{y ))}$ representing the individual scaling weight that gives higher weight to samples that are challenging for the model, $\mathsf{s ^{G (a) = \frac{\exp\left(\mathsf{o (\mathcal{L ,\mathcal{L _a) \right) {\sum_{g \in \mathcal{A \exp\left(\mathsf{o (\mathcal{L ,\mathcal{L _g) \right)}$ denoting the group scaling weight that promotes balanced learning across groups by computing the optimal transport function $\mathsf{o (\mathcal{L ,\mathcal{L _g)$ that measures the distance between group loss distribution $\mathcal{L _g = \{\ell(h_{\phi \left ( f_{\theta (x)\right), y)\}_{(x,y,a) \in \mathcal{D |a=g}$ and the overall loss distribution $\mathcal{L = \{\ell(h_{\phi \left ( f_{\theta (x)\right), y)\}_{(x,y,a) \in \mathcal{D }$, and $c \in [0,1]$ being a hyper-parameter controlling the balance between individual and group scaling weights.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: The value of $c$ controls whether training prioritizes overall accuracy ($c \approx 0$) or fairness ($c \approx 1$) -- for this pre-training, we set $c=0.5$ for balanced training.
elements.sent-2411-063: The loss function in~\eqref{eq:fis_optimization automatically adjusts the sample weight $\mathsf{w (.)$ based on sample-specific and group-level losses to ensure that the backbone model learns an overall accurate and fair feature space produced by the backbone feature extractor $f_{\theta^{* (.)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: The cohort-specific teacher models are formed by adding a new randomly initialized classification layer $h_{\phi^{(t) _{g (.)$, for each of the teachers $g \in \{0,\cdots,A\}$, to the pre-trained backbone model $f_{\theta^{* (.)$ from~\eqref{eq:fis_optimization , and fine tune each model by greedily minimizing classification risk for each demographic cohort $g\in \{0,\cdots,A\}$, with the following loss: \begin{equation}0.85{$ \phi^{(t)^*}_{g}= \arg\min\limits_{\phi^{(t)}_{g}} \frac{1}{|\mathcal{D}_g|} \sum\limits_{(x,y,a) \in \mathcal{D}_g} \mathsf{w}(x,y,a,c) \times \ell \left(h_{\phi_{g}^{(t)}} \left (f_{\theta^{*}}(x),y \right ) \right), $} \label{eq:optimization_teacher}\end{equation} where \( \ell(h_{\phi \left ( f_{\theta (x)\right), y) \) is the classification loss, $\mathsf{w (x,y,a,c)$ is the sample weight defined in~\eqref{eq:fis_optimization with $c=0$ to focus exclusively on the greedy individual scaling, $\mathcal{D _g = \{(x,y,a) | (x,y,a) \in \mathcal{D , a=g\}$, and $|.|$ is the set cardinality operator.}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-066: This setup allows each teacher model to achieve high accuracy for its own demographic group, which is an essential step for the knowledge distillation detailed in the next section.

elements.sent-2411-070: By combining the knowledge distillation from the teacher models with the balanced fairness-accuracy FIS loss, the student model is optimized to enhance the Pareto front achieved by FIS.
elements.sent-2411-071: This approach ensures high overall accuracy, strong group-specific performance, and fair classification across groups.
elements.sent-2411-072: We evaluate FairDi on classification and segmentation tasks to showcase its versatility.
elements.sent-2411-073: This section outlines the experimental setup, datasets, evaluation metrics, competing methods, and statistical tests, followed by classification and segmentation results and an ablation study.
elements.sent-2411-074: Finally, we compare training and testing times.
elements.sent-2411-075: Training: The classification experiments use the ResNet18~\cite{he2016deep backbone.}
elements.sent-2411-076: In Step 0, we use Adam optimizer, a weight decay of $1\times10^{-4}$, and an initial learning rate of $1\times10^{-4}$ that is decayed by a factor of $0.1$ every $10$ epochs, for a total of $30$ epochs.
elements.sent-2411-077: To prevent overfitting, we apply an early stopping criterion where training halts if the validation worst-case area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) does not improve over $5$ consecutive epochs.
elements.sent-2411-078: For Step 1, the teacher models are initialized from this backbone, with the final classification layer being randomly initialized.
elements.sent-2411-079: For training the teacher models, we use the SGD optimizer with a momentum of $0.9$.
elements.sent-2411-080: Early stopping is applied here as well, halting training if the respective cohort validation AUC does not improve over $5$ consecutive epochs.
elements.sent-2411-081: For Step 2, the student model is initialized from the backbone model with a randomly initialized classification layer, and is trained using an SGD optimizer with a momentum of $0.9$.
elements.sent-2411-082: The distillation loss is configured with a temperature value of \textcolor{blue {$\tau=1.5$} in \cref{eq:optimization_student to soften the teacher logits.}}
elements.sent-2411-083: Early stopping is applied to the student model based on the validation worst-case AUC, with training terminating if no improvement is observed over $5$ epochs.
elements.sent-2411-084: We use CutMix~\cite{yun2019cutmix to augment the training data for the training of backbone, teacher and student models.}
elements.sent-2411-085: Our experiments are conducted on a computer using one NVIDIA RTX A6000 GPU.
elements.sent-2411-086: The implementation is built on Python $3.12.7$ and PyTorch $2.2.2$.
elements.sent-2411-087: The segmentation experiments utilize the TransUNet backbone, built on the ViT-B (Vision Transformer) architecture, tailored specifically for medical imaging.
elements.sent-2411-088: The model is trained using the AdamW optimizer with a learning rate of 0.01, momentum of 0.9, weight decay of \(1 \times 10^{-4}\), and exponential learning rate decay.
elements.sent-2411-089: In the initial stages of training, a warmup strategy is employed to stabilize model training with the Harvard-FairSeg dataset~\cite{tian2024fairseg .}
elements.sent-2411-090: The TransUNet model is trained for 300 epochs without early stopping, following its original implementation, ensuring robust segmentation performance.
elements.sent-2411-091: The batch size for training is set to 42.
elements.sent-2411-092: The warmup strategy is applied at the beginning of training to stabilize the models.
elements.sent-2411-093: Datasets: For the classification task, we conduct our experiments on five medical imaging datasets that have been used to benchmark fairness methods~\cite{zong2022medfair --they are: Fitzpatrick17K~\citep{groh2021evaluating , HAM10000~\cite{tschandl2018ham10000 , Papila~\cite{kovalyk2022papila , CheXpert~\cite{irvin2019chexpert , and MIMIC-CXR~\cite{johnson2019mimic .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-094: To ensure consistent data preparation across all datasets, we follow a standardized pre-processing approach inspired by~\cite{zong2022medfair .}
elements.sent-2411-095: For example, we binarize sensitive attributes (e.g., skin type, age, sex, and race) and classification labels to facilitate evaluation of demographic fairness.
elements.sent-2411-096: More specifically, HAM10000 has classes benign and malignant, and two sensitive attributes: ‘Age’ (binarized into 0 to 60 and above 60) and ‘Gender’ (Male and Female).
elements.sent-2411-097: Fitzpatrick17k has classes malignant and non-malignant, with the ‘Skin Type’ attribute binarized into one group with skin types ranging from 0 to 2, and another group with skin types above 2.
elements.sent-2411-098: The PAPILA dataset has classes healthy and glaucoma, with two sensitive attributes: ‘Age’ (binarized into 0 to 60 and above 60) and ‘Gender’ (Male and Female).
elements.sent-2411-099: The CheXpert and MIMIC-CXR has classes 'No-Finding' and 'Any Finding', with three sensitive attributes: ‘Age’ (binarized into 0 to 60 and above 60), ‘Gender’ (Male and Female), and ‘Race’ (Non-White, including Black or Asian patients, and White).
elements.sent-2411-100: Additionally, as instructed by the MedFair benchmark~\cite{zong2022medfair , samples with incomplete or missing data are excluded from the analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-101: A more detailed description of the pre-processing is provided in Sec.~\ref{sec:data_preprocessing of the supplementary material.}
elements.sent-2411-102: For the segmentation task, we use the Harvard-FairSeg dataset~\cite{tian2024fairseg , which is a scanning laser ophthalmoscopy (SLO) fundus image dataset with a disc-cup segmentation task to assess the optic nerve head structures and diagnosing glaucoma in its early stage.}
elements.sent-2411-103: This Harvard-FairSeg dataset has 10,000 samples collected from 10,000 unique subjects.
elements.sent-2411-104: We split the data into an 8,000 for training and a 2,000 for testing.
elements.sent-2411-105: The subjects in the dataset have a mean age of 60.3 years, with a standard deviation of 16.5 years.
elements.sent-2411-106: Six sensitive attributes—age, gender, race, ethnicity, preferred language, and marital status—are available to support fairness-focused studies.
elements.sent-2411-107: In this paper, we focus on three sensitive attributes with the highest observed group-wise discrepancies: race, gender, and ethnicity.
elements.sent-2411-108: More details about the classification and segmentation datasets are shown in~\cref{sec:dataset_details in the supp.. material.}
elements.sent-2411-109: Evaluation Measures and Statistical Tests: Binary classification is assessed with AUC.
elements.sent-2411-110: To evaluate fairness, we report metrics that account for performance disparities across demographics cohorts.
elements.sent-2411-111: For instance, AUC Gap~\cite{zong2022medfair measures the performance difference between the highest and lowest-performing cohorts.}
elements.sent-2411-112: The Worst-Case AUC~\cite{zong2022medfair calculates the minimum AUC achieved among all cohorts.}
elements.sent-2411-113: Another fairness measure that we present is the Equity-Scaled AUC (ES-AUC)~\cite{luo2024fairvisionequitabledeeplearning , defined in Sec.~\ref{sec:fairness_measures of the supplementary material, which adjusts the overall AUC by penalizing discrepancies between the AUCs of the cohorts.}}
elements.sent-2411-114: We also measure the Performance-Scaled Disparity (PSD) that evaluates fairness by comparing cohort performance relative to overall AUC--please see Sec.~\ref{sec:fairness_measures of the supplementary material for the definition of the MeanPSD, which calculates the standard deviation of AUC scores across cohorts, and MaxPSD that calculates the maximum absolute difference between cohorts' AUCs.}
elements.sent-2411-115: We evaluate the segmentation tasks using the Dice Similarity Coefficient~\cite{dice1945measures,sorensen1948method , which measures the overlap between predicted and ground truth segmentations, and the Intersection over Union (IoU)~\cite{jaccard1901etude that measures the ratio of the intersection to the union of the predicted and ground truth segments.}}
elements.sent-2411-116: In the FairSeg benchmark~\cite{tian2024fairseg , Equity-scaled Dice (ES-Dice) and IoU (ES-IoU) modify the Dice and IoU to penalize discrepancies in performance across demographic groups by scaling the overlap scores down if subgroup performance is uneven.}
elements.sent-2411-117: In essence, equity-scaled metrics reduce the overall score when there are larger performance gaps between cohorts, encouraging models to achieve consistent segmentation quality across all subgroups--please see Sec.~\ref{sec:fairness_measures of the supplementary material for the definition of ES-Dice and ES-IoU.}
elements.sent-2411-118: As suggested in~\cite{zong2022medfair , we test significance by applying the Friedman test~\cite{friedman1937use , followed by the Nemenyi post-hoc test~\cite{nemenyi1963distribution , to detect significant performance differences between algorithms.}}}
elements.sent-2411-119: If the Friedman test shows significance (p-value $< 0.05$), the Nemenyi test averages the ranks of each algorithm across datasets.
elements.sent-2411-120: The results are displayed using Critical Difference (CD) diagrams, where algorithms connected by a line are statistically similar, while those in separate groups are significantly different.
elements.sent-2411-121: Baselines and State-of-the-art (SOTA) Methods: We compare our proposed FairDi against several baseline and SOTA fairness methods.
elements.sent-2411-122: For the classification task, a common baseline in fairness studies is the Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM)~\cite{vapnik1999overview , which is trained by minimizing the average classification error across the training set without accounting for demographic cohorts or sensitive attributes.}
elements.sent-2411-123: While ERM optimizes overall accuracy, it overlooks group-specific biases, making it a valuable baseline for assessing fairness gaps.
elements.sent-2411-124: GroupDRO~\cite{sagawa2019distributionally is designed to minimize the worst-case loss across demographic groups.}
elements.sent-2411-125: By focusing on the group with the highest training loss, GroupDRO emphasizes performance fairness, aiming to protect disadvantaged cohorts.
elements.sent-2411-126: Another baseline, SWAD~\cite{cha2021swad , explores a range of flat minima in the loss landscape through dense sampling of model weights.}
elements.sent-2411-127: This approach is particularly beneficial for enhancing model generalization across diverse settings, but does not directly address fairness across demographic groups.
elements.sent-2411-128: We also compare our approach to FIS~\cite{luo2024fairvisionequitabledeeplearning , the SOTA fairness method that applies a scaling mechanism to adjust loss contributions based on demographic cohort performance.}
elements.sent-2411-129: We evaluate fair segmentation with the TransUNet backbone~\cite{chen2021transunet , which combines CNNs with transformers, leveraging self-attention for capturing long-range dependencies while preserving spatial hierarchies, making it effective for medical image segmentation.}
elements.sent-2411-130: The first TransUNet-based fair segmentation method is implemented by combining it with the Adversarially Fair Representations (ADV)~\cite{madras2018learning to create unbiased representations by making it difficult to infer sensitive attributes, thus reducing bias.}
elements.sent-2411-131: Another fair TransUNet method is achieved by combining it with GroupDRO~\cite{sagawa2019distributionally , which improves model fairness by minimizing the maximum training loss across groups, using regularization to prevent bias toward any single group.}
elements.sent-2411-132: We also compare with the SOTA TransUNet+Fair Error-Bound Scaling (FEBS)~\cite{tian2024fairseg that improves fairness by rescaling the loss based on each identity group’s upper training error bound.}

elements.sent-2411-150: In this ablation study, we explore the technical contributions of our FairDi model, on two classification datasets, to understand their impact on accuracy and fairness.
elements.sent-2411-151: Tab.~\ref{tab:ablation presents the results across the HAM10000 and PAPILA datasets, focusing on Gender as the sensitive attribute.}
elements.sent-2411-152: We start from the baseline ERM model (ResNet18) trained with binary cross entropy (BCE) loss~\cite{zong2022medfair , which has moderate Overall AUC scores of $0.8890$ on HAM10000 and $0.8433$ on PAPILA.}
elements.sent-2411-153: Integrating CutMix~\cite{yun2019cutmix , the Overall AUC results improve to $0.8952$ on HAM10000 and $0.9142$ on PAPILA.}
elements.sent-2411-154: However, this accuracy enhancement results in a larger AUC Gap, particularly on PAPILA, which reaches $0.1500$.
elements.sent-2411-155: Replacing BCE with FIS loss~\cite{luo2024fairvisionequitabledeeplearning , increases Overall AUC to $0.9174$ on HAM10000 and $0.8938$ on PAPILA and substantially reduces AUC Gap to $0.0071$ on HAM10000 and $0.0331$ on PAPILA, showing a better balance between accuracy and fairness.}
elements.sent-2411-156: The combination of FIS Loss with CutMix leads to further improvements, reaching an Overall AUC of $0.9300$ on HAM10000 and $0.9391$ on PAPILA and further AUC Gap reductions to $0.0011$ on HAM10000 and $0.0285$ on PAPILA.
elements.sent-2411-157: This configuration achieves high ES-AUC and low MeanPSD and MaxPSD values, indicating a favorable balance between accuracy and fairness.
elements.sent-2411-158: This forms the backbone model from Step 0, explained in Sec.~\ref{sec:training_step_0 .}
elements.sent-2411-159: Step 1, explained in Sec.~\ref{sec:training_step_1 , consists of training group-specific FairDi-teacher models, which have high group-specific overall AUC scores, with Female AUC reaching $0.9585$ and Male AUC reaching $0.9510$ on HAM10000, and similar performances on PAPILA (Female AUC of $0.9515$ and Male AUC of $0.9841$).}
elements.sent-2411-160: However, these biased models also introduce larger AUC Gap values ($0.0285$ on HAM10000 and $0.0814$ on PAPILA) due to the inherent performance discrepancy between the cohort-specific models.
elements.sent-2411-161: This suggests that while group-specific performances are strong, fairness is sacrificed.
elements.sent-2411-162: The final knowledge distillation (from group-specific teacher models) and accuracy-fairness balanced training of the student model during Step 2, explained in Sec.~\ref{sec:training_step_2 , produces the FairDi-student model, which achieves an Overall AUC of $0.9560$ on HAM10000 and $0.9572$ on PAPILA.}
elements.sent-2411-163: Both Female and Male AUCs remain high, while the model minimizes the AUC Gap to just $0.000029$ on HAM10000 and $0.0349$ on PAPILA.
elements.sent-2411-164: These results indicate a significant reduction in cohort-specific performance disparities compared to the biased teacher models, demonstrating the effectiveness of our FairDi design in balancing cohort-specific performance with overall accuracy and fairness.
elements.sent-2411-165: For the HAM10000 dataset with gender-sensitive attribute, the training times for ERM, SWAD, GroupDRO, and FIS were 19.75, 28.78, 19.12, and 17.56 minutes, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-166: In comparison, the FairDi method’s total training time is structured across the following multiple steps: Backbone Model Training (Step 0) takes 17.56 minutes, Teacher (Female) Model Training (Step 1) takes 5.53 minutes, Teacher (Male) Model Training (Step 1) takes 4.26 minutes, and Student Model Training (Step 2) takes 16.96 minutes, resulting in a combined training time of $\approx 40$ minutes, assuming that the two teachers can be trained in parallel.
elements.sent-2411-167: The testing time for ERM, SWAD, GroupDRO, FIS, and FairDi is identical at 0.033 seconds per image.
elements.sent-2411-168: In terms of memory footprint, the backbone model contains a total of $\approx$11M parameters, all of which are trainable at Step 0 of the training.
elements.sent-2411-169: The classification layers of the female and male teacher models introduce $\approx$0.5K (each) new parameters trainable at Step 1 of the training.
elements.sent-2411-170: Similarly, the student model adds another $\approx$0.5K new parameters at the classification layer, with the same $\approx$11M non-trainable parameters from the backbone model.
elements.sent-2411-171: In this work, we introduced FairDi, a novel fairness method that tackles the challenging trade-offs between accuracy, subgroup-specific performance, and fairness in medical imaging.
elements.sent-2411-172: Unlike traditional fairness approaches that attempt to balance these competing goals within a single model, FairDi leverages the strengths of biased teacher models trained specifically for sensitive subgroups.
elements.sent-2411-173: This unique strategy allows the unified student model to distill the teacher models' knowledge and achieve high overall accuracy, while reducing performance disparities across demographic groups.
elements.sent-2411-174: Our extensive evaluations across multiple medical imaging datasets demonstrate that FairDi consistently outperforms current fairness methods, achieving both high accuracy and fairness.
elements.sent-2411-175: The experimental results highlight FairDi's ability to narrow the AUC gap between advantaged and disadvantaged subgroups, while also minimizing performance-scaled disparity (PSD), a crucial metric for real-world clinical relevance.
elements.sent-2411-176: Furthermore, by distilling knowledge from subgroup-optimized teachers, FairDi not only enhances the interpretability of fairness outcomes but also sets a new standard for equitable model performance in healthcare.
elements.sent-2411-177: The flexibility and adaptability of FairDi make it applicable to a range of medical tasks, including classification and segmentation, underscoring its potential impact on the design of fair AI systems in critical domains.
elements.sent-2411-178: As healthcare increasingly adopts AI solutions, FairDi stands as a promising pathway toward ethical, inclusive, and high-performing models that prioritize equitable outcomes for all patients.
elements.sent-2411-179: We hope that this work paves the way for further advancements in fairness-centered AI, inspiring new strategies that embrace the complexities of fairness without sacrificing performance.
elements.sent-2411-180: The data preprocessing in this work follows the approach outlined in~\cite{zong2022medfair .. For data splitting.}
elements.sent-2411-181: The dataset is randomly partitioned into training, validation, and testing sets, maintaining an $80/10/10$ ratio unless stated otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-182: We then binarize both the prediction labels and sensitive attributes.
elements.sent-2411-183: Further details on this process are provided in the next section.
elements.sent-2411-184: In this section, we provide details about the datasets.
elements.sent-2411-185: All datasets are publicly available and can be accessed through the URLs listed in Table~\ref{tab:dataset_access .}
elements.sent-2411-186: The dataset statistics are provided in Table~\ref{tab:dataset_statistics .}
elements.sent-2411-187: Additionally, we summarize the statistics for the subgroups and class labels in Tables~\ref{tab:ham10000_label_distribution through \ref{tab:mimic_cxr_label_distribution .}}
elements.sent-2411-188: For the classification datasets, the sensitive attributes are defined as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-189: Skin Type: For the sensitive attribute of skin type in Fitzpatrick17k, we divided the data into two groups.
elements.sent-2411-190: The first group includes samples with skin types ranging from 0 to 2, while the second group consists of samples with skin types above 2.
elements.sent-2411-191: Age: For HAM10000, PAPILA, CheXpert, and MIMIC-CXR datasets, we classified the sensitive attribute of age into two distinct categories: the first group includes individuals aged 0 to 60, while the second group consists of those older than 60.
elements.sent-2411-192: Race: For CheXpert and MIMIC-CXR datasets, we defined two groups of race: a non-White group, comprising patients identified as 'Black' or 'Asian,' and a second group of patients identified as 'White'.
elements.sent-2411-193: Gender: For HAM10000, PAPILA, CheXpert, and MIMIC-CXR datasets, the sensitive attribute of gender has two categories: the first group consists of 'Male' patients, while the second group includes 'Female' patients.
elements.sent-2411-194: The Harvard-FairSeg dataset~\cite{tian2024fairseg encompasses six distinct sensitive attributes.}
elements.sent-2411-195: Specifically, regarding racial demographics, it includes samples from three primary groups: Asian (919 samples), Black (1,473 samples), and White (7,608 samples).
elements.sent-2411-196: Gender distribution shows that females represent 58.5\% of subjects, with males comprising the remaining 41.5\%.
elements.sent-2411-197: Ethnically, 90.6\% of participants are Non-Hispanic, 3.7\% are Hispanic, and 5.7\% are unspecified.
elements.sent-2411-198: For preferred language, 92.4\% of individuals prefer English, 1.5\% prefer Spanish, 1\% prefer other languages, and 5.1\% have no specified language preference.
elements.sent-2411-199: Regarding marital status, 57.7\% are married or partnered, 27.1\% are single, 6.8\% are divorced, 0.8\% are legally separated, 5.2\% are widowed, and 2.4\% did not specify.
elements.sent-2411-200: In this study, we focus on the three sensitive attributes with the highest observed group-wise disparities: race, gender, and ethnicity.
elements.sent-2411-201: Fitzpatrick17k: We grouped the three partition labels into binary categories, namely benign and malignant.
elements.sent-2411-202: In this grouping, we classified both non-neoplastic and benign under the benign category, while malignant remained as the malignant category.
elements.sent-2411-203: Furthermore, we used Fitzpatrick skin type labels as the sensitive attributes for our analysis.
elements.sent-2411-204: HAM10000: We followed the approach by~\cite{maron2019systematic to categorize the seven diagnostic labels into two binary groups: benign and malignant.. \begin{itemize.}}
elements.sent-2411-205: The benign group includes basal cell carcinoma (bcc), benign keratosis-like lesions (e.g., solar lentigines, seborrheic keratoses, and lichen-planus-like keratoses, bkl), dermatofibroma (df), melanocytic nevi (nv), and vascular lesions (angiomas, angiokeratomas, pyogenic granulomas, and hemorrhage, vasc).
elements.sent-2411-206: The malignant group consists of actinic keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma (Bowen's disease, akiec) as well as melanoma (mel).
elements.sent-2411-207: Images without recorded sensitive attributes were excluded from the dataset, leaving a total of 9,948 images.
elements.sent-2411-208: The dataset contains both right-eye and left-eye images from the same patients.
elements.sent-2411-209: To divide the dataset into training, validation, and test sets, we followed a specific ratio: 70\% for training, 10\% for validation, and 20\% for testing.
elements.sent-2411-210: Importantly, we ensured that images from the same patient are not shared across these splits.
elements.sent-2411-211: This practice preserves the independence of the data subsets, which is essential for accurately evaluating the model’s performance.
elements.sent-2411-212: Each image may be assigned one or more labels from a set of 14, representing various clinical observations.
elements.sent-2411-213: During preprocessing, we excluded images that lacked sensitive attribute labels.
elements.sent-2411-214: The No Finding label is utilized for both training and testing purposes.
elements.sent-2411-215: All available frontal and lateral images were included, ensuring that images from the same patient were not shared across the training, validation, and test sets, following the approach outlined by~\cite{zong2022medfair .}
elements.sent-2411-216: We merged this data with the original MIMIC-CXR metadata using the subject ID as the key.
elements.sent-2411-217: Other preprocessing steps closely follow the procedures used in the CheXpert dataset.. \end{itemize.}

elements.sent-2411-218: For the classification metrics, the ES-AUC provides an equity-focused assessment by scaling the overall AUC based on performance differences across subgroups, calculated as follows: \begin{equation}\mathsf{ES-AUC} = \frac{\mathsf{AUC}(\mathcal{D})}{1 + \frac{1}{A} \sum_{g \in \mathcal{A}} \left| \mathsf{AUC}(\mathcal{D}) - \mathsf{AUC}(\mathcal{D}_g) \right|} \label{eq:ES-AUC}\end{equation} where $\mathsf{AUC (\mathcal{D )$ represents the overall AUC for the whole test set, $g \in \mathcal{A \subset \{0,\cdots,A\}$ represents the value of the identity attribute (e.g., for the gender attribute, the values can be male or female), $\mathsf{AUC (\mathcal{D _g)$ represents the cohort-specific AUC on the test set $\mathcal{D _g = \{(x,y,a) | (x,y,a) \in \mathcal{D , a=g\}$ defined in~\eqref{eq:optimization_teacher .}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-219: This measure promotes fairness by proportionally reducing the overall AUC in response to increased subgroup disparities.
elements.sent-2411-220: The Performance-Scaled Disparity (PSD) evaluates fairness by comparing cohort performance relative to overall AUC.
elements.sent-2411-221: Mean PSD calculates the standard deviation of AUC scores across cohorts, while Max PSD calculates the maximum absolute difference between cohorts' AUCs, both scaled by the overall AUC.
elements.sent-2411-222: These metrics are defined as follows: \begin{equation}\mathsf{Mean PSD}=\frac{\sqrt{\frac{1}{A}\sum_{g\in \mathcal{A}}\left(\mathsf{AUC}(\mathcal{D}_g)-\mathsf{AUC}_{\text{mean}}\right)^2}}{\mathsf{AUC}(\mathcal{D})}, \label{eq:Mean-PSD}\end{equation} where $\mathsf{AUC _{\text{mean = \frac{1 {A \sum_{g\in \mathcal{A \mathsf{AUC (\mathcal{D _g)$, and \begin{equation}\mathsf{Max PSD} =\frac{\left( \max_{g \in \mathcal{A}}(\mathsf{AUC}(\mathcal{D}_g))-\min_{g \in \mathcal{A}}(\mathsf{AUC}(\mathcal{D}_g))\right)}{\mathsf{AUC}(\mathcal{D})} \label{eq:Max-PSD}.\end{equation}.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-223: These PSD measures provide interpretable insights into the model’s fairness performance, especially in clinical contexts where consistent accuracy across demographic groups is essential.
elements.sent-2411-224: For the segmentation measures, the Dice coeffcient is calculated as~\cite{dice1945measures,sorensen1948method \begin{equation}\mathsf{Dice}(y,\hat{y}) = \frac{2 \times |y \cap \hat{y}|}{|y| + |\hat{y}|},\end{equation} where \(y\) and \(\hat{y}\) represent the actual and predicted segmented pixels, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-225: The Intersection over Union (IoU)~\cite{jaccard1901etude is given by \begin{equation}\mathsf{IoU}(y,\hat{y}) = \frac{|y \cap \hat{y}|}{|y \cup \hat{y}|}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-226: Both metrics range from 0 to 1, with 1 indicating perfect overlap, but Dice tends to be slightly more sensitive to small overlaps than IoU due to its formula.
elements.sent-2411-227: To adapt Dice to penalize discrepancies in performance across demographic groups, we first define a performance discrepancy as follows: \begin{equation}\Delta(y,\hat{y}) = \sum_{g \in \mathcal{A}} \left | \mathsf{Dice}(y,\hat{y}) - \mathsf{Dice}(y,\hat{y};g) \right |, \label{eq:Delta}\end{equation} where $\mathsf{Dice (y,\hat{y ;g)$ measures Dice for samples belonging to a particular value $g \in \mathcal{A}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-228: The metric $\Delta(y,\hat{y )$ quantifies the deviation in performance of each demographic group relative to the overall performance, approaching zero when all groups achieve comparable segmentation accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-229: To evaluate fairness across different groups, we compute the relative disparity between the overall segmentation accuracy and the accuracy within each demographic group.
elements.sent-2411-230: The Equity-Scaled Dice (ES-Dice) metric is then defined as: \begin{equation}\mathsf{ES-Dice}(y,\hat{y}) = \frac{\mathsf{Dice}(y,\hat{y})}{1 + \Delta(y,\hat{y})}. \label{eq:ES-Dice}\end{equation} This formulation ensures that $\mathsf{ES-Dice (.)$ is always less than or equal to 1.}
elements.sent-2411-231: As $\Delta$ decreases (indicating more equitable segmentation performance across groups), $\mathsf{ES-Dice (.)$ converges to $\mathsf{Dice (.)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-232: Conversely, a higher $\Delta$ reflects greater disparity in segmentation performance across demographics, leading to a lower $\mathsf{ES-Dice (.)$ score.}
elements.sent-2411-233: This approach enables us to assess segmentation models not only for accuracy (measured by Dice) but also for fairness across demographic groups.
elements.sent-2411-234: Note that the calculation of $\mathsf{ES-IoU (.)$ follows the same steps as defined in~\eqref{eq:Delta and~\eqref{eq:ES-Dice .}}}

elements.sent-2411-235: After receiving paper reviews, authors may optionally submit a rebuttal to address the reviewers' comments, which will be limited to a {\bf one page PDF file.}
elements.sent-2411-236: Please follow the steps and style guidelines outlined below for submitting your author response.
elements.sent-2411-237: The author rebuttal is optional and, following similar guidelines to previous conferences, is meant to provide you with an opportunity to rebut factual errors or to supply additional information requested by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-238: It is NOT intended to add new contributions (theorems, algorithms, experiments) that were absent in the original submission and NOT specifically requested by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-239: You may optionally add a figure, graph, or proof to your rebuttal to better illustrate your answer to the reviewers' comments.
elements.sent-2411-240: Per a passed 2018 PAMI-TC motion, reviewers should refrain from requesting significant additional experiments for the rebuttal or penalize for lack of additional experiments.
elements.sent-2411-241: Authors should refrain from including new experimental results in the rebuttal, especially when not specifically requested to do so by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-242: Authors may include figures with illustrations or comparison tables of results reported in the submission/supplemental material or in other papers.
elements.sent-2411-243: Just like the original submission, the rebuttal must maintain anonymity and cannot include external links that reveal the author identity or circumvent the length restriction.
elements.sent-2411-244: The rebuttal must comply with this template (the use of sections is not required, though it is recommended to structure the rebuttal for ease of reading).
elements.sent-2411-245: Author responses must be no longer than 1 page in length including any references and figures.
elements.sent-2411-246: Overlength responses will simply not be reviewed.
elements.sent-2411-247: This includes responses where the margins and formatting are deemed to have been significantly altered from those laid down by this style guide.
elements.sent-2411-248: Note that this \LaTeX\ guide already sets figure captions and references in a smaller font.
elements.sent-2411-249: All text must be in a two-column format.
elements.sent-2411-250: The total allowable size of the text area is $6\frac78$ inches (17.46 cm) wide by $8\frac78$ inches (22.54 cm) high.
elements.sent-2411-251: Columns are to be $3\frac14$ inches (8.25 cm) wide, with a $\frac{5 {16}$ inch (0.8 cm) space between them.}
elements.sent-2411-252: The top margin should begin 1 inch (2.54 cm) from the top edge of the page.
elements.sent-2411-253: The bottom margin should be $1\frac{1 {8}$ inches (2.86 cm) from the bottom edge of the page for $8.5 \times 11$-inch paper; for A4 paper, approximately $1\frac{5 {8}$ inches (4.13 cm) from the bottom edge of the page.}}
elements.sent-2411-255: It is important for readers to be able to refer to any particular equation.
elements.sent-2411-256: Wherever Times is specified, Times Roman may also be used.
elements.sent-2411-257: Main text should be in 10-point Times, single-spaced.
elements.sent-2411-258: Section headings should be in 10 or 12 point Times.
elements.sent-2411-259: All paragraphs should be indented 1 pica (approx.~$\frac{1 {6}$ inch or 0.422 cm).}
elements.sent-2411-260: Figure and table captions should be 9-point Roman type as in \cref{fig:onecol .}
elements.sent-2411-261: List and number all bibliographical references in 9-point Times, single-spaced, at the end of your response.
elements.sent-2411-262: When referenced in the text, enclose the citation number in square brackets, for example~\cite{Alpher05 .}
elements.sent-2411-263: Where appropriate, include the name(s) of editors of referenced books.

elements.sent-2411-264: To avoid ambiguities, it is best if the numbering for equations, figures, tables, and references in the author response does not overlap with that in the main paper (the reviewer may wonder if you talk about \cref{fig:onecol in the author response or in the paper).}
elements.sent-2411-267: Please ensure that any point you wish to make is resolvable in a printed copy of the response.
elements.sent-2411-268: Resize fonts in figures to match the font in the body text, and choose line widths which render effectively in print.
elements.sent-2411-269: Readers (and reviewers), even of an electronic copy, may choose to print your response in order to read it.
elements.sent-2411-270: You cannot insist that they do otherwise, and therefore must not assume that they can zoom in to see tiny details on a graphic.
